speaker: interviewer
text: What we are looking at in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are looking at a picture which I found in the Auschwitz Einz, number one, Museum in the Czech block. It's taken approximately in May, 1944. It's on the ramp of Birkenau-Auschwitz. It's an oncoming-- incoming Hungarian transport, and we were sent to bring some medical supplies from the train. The commando is walking directly through the prisoners, in front of the SS men, which, as well, might be Mengele. I don't remember. But I am there on the picture in the front-- looking for me from the left corner, it is being circled. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's all what I remember about it. I-- I don't remember being taken for the picture. It must have been taken by an SS Man.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken by an unknown British soldier on the 16 of April, 1945. It shows [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an unconscious young woman stricken by typhoid, lying there, forlorn. We found it in a Belgian museum as a blow-up, and it's Eva [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was found there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is an arrow in about the middle of that photograph. That's the room in which Eva stayed in Terezin before she was transported to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is in Theresienstadt, in the walls of the fortress where we had to chisel out a window to comply with the regulations. And we were four guys living in it, before my transportation. Before actually, I moved somewhere else, while being in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the registry index card which is at the Jewish Community in Prague. And there is-- on the right side will be the translation of it in English. Stating simply that I left for Terezin on the 24th of 10 in 1942 by the transport by the name of CA Number 740, and left Terezin for Auschwitz with the transport which is mentioned there. And in the right corner up is mentioned that I reported back to the registry, meaning I returned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture from one of the walls of Pinkas Synagogue in Prague, where the bare white walls bear the names of nearly 90,000 Czech Moravian Jews who died in concentration camps during the-- Hitler's period. And the mortality-- general mortality-- was about 88% of all the population of the people of Jewish origin living in these countries. There in the middle, one could see the name of Bertold, born 13, five, '86. That's my father. Next to him is actually my uncle and my aunt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a map of the camp in Schwarzheide, which shows the barracks on the left side and the trench I mentioned. And then on top, there is a gas-- small gas chamber prepared for us, which was never used. The-- the Germans, then, show it on a memorial, which I'll show on the next picture. And then on the right side of that is a route of our march up to Prague, which we marched up to the point where it's written, Wansdorf. And from there, we went with the train to Terezin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a memorial plaque which is-- is in the place called Schwarzheide, which was built by the Germans in memoriam of the people who stayed there as prisoners and who died there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is another memorial plaque from the same place, recognizing the Death March from Schwarzheide to Theresienstadt, which started on 18 four 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is another memorial plaque on which translation says that on this place was standing the gas chamber of the side camp, Schwarzheide, 1944-'45, which luckily was not used due to the fact that the war was rapidly coming to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These shoes are part of the memorabilia which we keep in one special little suitcase. These are the shoes in which I marched from Schwarzheide and was liberated. Then I came to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many kilometers did you walk in those shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked-- must have walked in it for quite a while, actually, in the camp. But then it was over 350 kilometers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is from the Index lexicon, meaning the roster of the lectures which had to be stamped for each semester when I started to study medicine in Prague. I used to be younger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A promotion of the book we wrote with Eva together about our war experiences. This is from the University of Texas journal. And we are, at that point, signing and trying to promote the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very rainy day and we are, in 1988, standing on the space of B2B Camp in Auschwitz-Birkenau. There were only remnants of the chimneys around us, and remnants of the barbed wire. In the background, then, there is the memorial and the dismantled-- the remnants of the dismantled gas chambers and crematoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a family picture on the anniversary of the 50 years wedding, so it's old, about three years. There is one grandchild missing, which we'll fill in in later pictures. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the picture of my oldest son and his family. It's about a couple of years old, but they all look at it quite well-- on it quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my middle son, Peter, and the granddaughter, Daniella. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That is my youngest, Misha, and her family-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] husband, Scott, Noah, and Sasha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Eva and I in Schwarzheide, which the city keeps as a memorial sacred grounds, for it burned down the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken, Dr. Roden?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken two years ago when-- Actually, last year, when I tried to retrace our Death March.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every year, I celebrate my birthday with a party in our country place in Eastern Townships. And these are the surviving co-prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us who you see in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of them is Fred Cohen, Joe Herman, Joe Wesley, I, Charlie Herman, and Frank Horsky. They all are very, very good friends of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
